questions.text: I sense when the real decision-maker in a meeting is not the person with the highest title.
I am the person who usually decides how resources get distributed in a group project.
I naturally consolidate decision-making power as projects progress.
I find myself naturally taking on extra work so that others don't have to.
I hold the view that my low reactivity is not a deficit — it's a different way of being in the world.
I experience the act of letting go of something as a natural, uncomplicated process.
I naturally listen first and speak after others have shared their thoughts.
I do not naturally track the ebb and flow of conversational dynamics.
My emotional landscape has extreme peaks and valleys rather than rolling hills.
I feel most productive when I have the freedom to shift direction at any moment.
I believe that pursuing personal excellence is a worthy way to spend my life.
I rarely notice the implicit choreography of how a group arranges itself when it walks together.
I tend to evaluate any group activity by asking what I personally stand to gain.
I am the person others call when something needs broad buy-in.
I feel that the moral pull of another person's pain is the most honest signal I have about what I truly value.
I often feel flooded by the intensity of my own reactions.
I tend to be unaware of the unspoken rules about what topics are acceptable at a particular gathering.
I respect the boundaries of my own schedule and protect time for rest and recovery.
I feel that gathering broad input helps reassure the people around me.
I sense when someone introduces a topic casually but actually considers it extremely important.
I prefer handling each situation freshly rather than automating it into a routine.
I prefer to experiment freely, using recipes and formulas only as loose guidelines.
I feel that contributing to a shared meal is more fulfilling than dining alone on something better.
I believe that developing a signature style—in work, dress, and communication—is important for my identity.
I sense the social temperature of an email based on word choice, punctuation, and formatting.
I hold the view that discipline is freedom, because it eliminates the chaos of indecision.
I find that the moral pull of suffering has been the defining influence on the choices that matter most in my life.
I believe that the capacity for dispassionate analysis in the face of human tragedy is indispensable for systems designed to serve large populations.
I find that being thorough the first time saves me from having to redo work later.
I hold the view that my weekends are better spent volunteering than relaxing.
My emotional responses feel like dial adjustments — small, precise increments rather than dramatic jumps.
I believe that objective assessment of emotional situations is the competence that most clearly distinguishes thoughtful intervention from reactive involvement.
I find that my ideas often shape the direction a group takes.
I hold the view that moral engagement with suffering means letting it influence the trajectory of my decisions, not just the color of my emotions.
I have been told that I seem unaffected by things that upset most people.
I have difficulty sleeping after an emotionally intense experience because the feelings won't settle.
